text: The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to meticulously dissect and remove dense adhesions, carefully teasing and manipulating the tissue to avoid damage to the delicate and closely adherent intestinal structures. The goal is to reach the gallbladder.
The gallbladder is visibly thickly adherent to the colon, omentum, and liver, with dense fibrous tissue connecting these structures.

The surgeon identifies pus emerging from the perforated side of the gallbladder, revealing the cavity filled with pus. Careful control is maintained to avoid damaging the liver and causing excessive bleeding.

The surgeon clears pus and purulent material from the surgical field, addressing the inflamed and adherent gallbladder. The gallbladder wall shows signs of inflammation and adhesions, complicating surgical maneuvers.

The surgeon attempts to identify the liver bed, noting the difficulty due to chronic inflammation. Using a fundus-first approach, the gallbladder fundus is demarcated and separated from surrounding tissues, aiding in the eventual access to Calot's triangle.

The surgeon attempts to identify the neck of the gallbladder, located opposite the basilar sulcus, to separate adhesions around the cystic duct. The cystic duct and artery are noted to have larger diameters than usual. The surgeon carefully separates the posterior relations of the cystic duct to prevent angulation and avoid damage to surrounding structures.

In the presence of chronic inflammation, the surgeon identifies accessory blood vessels and clamps and staples the cystic duct. The cystic duct stump is secured with a double V stapler, ensuring proper closure.

The surgeon staples the cystic artery, securing it with double staples due to its unusual diameter. Cold scissors are used to excise the cystic duct, with visible pulsations indicating its location amidst inflamed tissue.

The surgeon uses a monopolar hook to separate the remaining portion of the gallbladder from the liver bed, following a fundus-first approach. The gallbladder is partially intrahepatic, requiring careful dissection. The surgeon checks the staples applied to the cystic duct and artery to ensure they are secure.
In the laparoscopic view, the surgeon applies traction and counter-traction at a ninety-degree angle to the separation area, completing the dissection of the gallbladder from the liver bed.
